summary: My grandfather might have been a good model for Ado Annie's boyfriend in the musical Oklahoma! He came to Oklahoma in the late 1890s and worked as a peddler, moving from town to town as the booms started and tapered off. This connection to Oklahoma! reflects the kind of life and people he encountered in those early days in the state.

Maud was a sizeable town when my dad had his grocery store, Modern Grocery on Main Street, and later a large store called Adwan's General Merchandise. My sister was born there four years after I was, and my dad had a grocery store, department store, and a Frigidaire appliance franchise in Maud. It was a place where the boom had started to taper off but still had a community life that I was part of growing up.

My dad eventually moved to Seminole, Oklahoma, after his time running stores in Maud. I also worked at the Seminole Producer, the hometown newspaper, during my time as a student at the University of Oklahoma and after graduation. Seminole was an important place for me professionally and personally as it was my hometown and where I began my journalism career.

I vividly remember the day after Pearl Harbor was attacked in 1941. Mrs. Harris gathered us in the auditorium to hear President Roosevelt's Day of Infamy speech. I was only eleven, but that moment marked a turning point in our lives and the country's involvement in World War II.

I recall hearing President Franklin D. Roosevelt's Day of Infamy speech shortly after Pearl Harbor in 1941. It was a powerful moment that was broadcast to us at school, and it made a deep impression on me as a young boy. Roosevelt's leadership was a defining part of that era and shaped the course of the war and our national resolve.

I remember when Germany invaded Poland at the start of World War II. An old World War I veteran remarked that the Germans didn't know what they were in for because of the Polish cavalry, which was a bit of an outdated idea. That conversation stuck with me as a reflection of how people viewed the war early on.

World War I veterans often shared stories and perspectives that influenced how we thought about later conflicts. For example, the idea of cavalry charges was still talked about, even though it was unrealistic by World War II standards. Many men from Oklahoma, including some from the University of Oklahoma, served in World War I and went on to serve in later wars.

I attended the University of Oklahoma where I was involved in journalism, editing the school newspaper, the Guidon, and the yearbook, the Vedette. While a student, I worked at the Seminole Producer during summers and weekends, which helped me gain experience. OU was a formative place for me both academically and professionally.

I worked at the Seminole Producer, my hometown newspaper, starting as a student at the University of Oklahoma. One summer, probably around 1949, the publisher Milt Philips offered me a job on the payroll, and I worked there during summers and weekends. After graduation, I went to work there full-time, which was an important step in my journalism career.

We left New Orleans as part of my military deployment, traveling through the Panama Canal and then stopping briefly on the West Coast before heading to Japan. New Orleans was a departure point on this journey in 1951, marking the start of my overseas service during the Korean War.

Our troop transport went through the Panama Canal after leaving New Orleans on the way to Japan in 1951. It was a significant part of the journey from the United States to the Pacific theater during the Korean War. The canal was a key transit point connecting the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans for military movements.

We stopped briefly in San Francisco on the West Coast after passing through the Panama Canal and before heading to Japan. It was a short but memorable stop on the way to my deployment in the Korean War. San Francisco was a key port of embarkation for many troops heading to the Pacific.

In 1951, I trained on the west side of Japan, in a northernmost island called Okido. Japan was a major staging area for American forces during the Korean War, and we spent time there preparing before heading into combat. General Matthew Ridgway even came down from Tokyo to inspect our division while we were there.

Okido is a northernmost island in Japan where I trained with the 45th Infantry Division in 1951 before deploying to Korea. It was a key location for preparing troops for combat during the Korean War. The training there was intense and crucial for our readiness.

General Matthew Ridgway came down from Tokyo in 1951 to inspect the 45th Infantry Division while we were training in Japan. I remember him stopping to talk to one of my tank commanders about our tanks and maintenance issues. He was quite upset when he learned about our practice of cannibalizing parts from other tanks, which was common but frowned upon.

Tokyo was the location General Matthew Ridgway came from to inspect our division in Japan in 1951. It was the command center for American forces in the region during the Korean War. Our training base in Okido was under the broader command structure based in Tokyo.

I served with the 45th Infantry Division during the Korean War, training in Japan and then deploying to Korea in late 1951. We arrived in Inchon Harbor on New Year's Eve and operated along the front near Cheorwon. The division saw tough fighting, and I was involved in tank support and reconnaissance missions, including Task Force Hobart, our first offensive operation into enemy territory.

We arrived at Inchon Harbor on New Year's Eve, 1951, and our first view was of a small island called Wamido that had been stripped of trees by artillery. Inchon was a critical port and staging area during the Korean War, and we operated in the surrounding areas as part of the 45th Infantry Division. It was a place where the war's intensity was very evident.

Wamido is a small island near Inchon that we saw first when we arrived in Korea in 1951. It looked like a bald head with a few hairs sticking up, having been stripped of trees by artillery fire from the sea. It was the first objective taken at Inchon and a stark reminder of the war's destruction.

Cheorwon was a key location along the front where we went into line during the Korean War. It was an old town that had been heavily damaged and was known for the Americans losing it early in the war when General William F. Dean was captured. Our tanks supported South Korean troops in this area, and the fighting resembled World War I trench warfare.

General William F. Dean was the division commander who was captured early in the Korean War near Cheorwon. His capture was a significant event and is remembered as part of the difficult early fighting in that area. His leadership and fate were well known among those of us serving in the 45th Infantry Division.

I served in the Korean War starting in 1951 with the 45th Infantry Division. We trained in Japan, landed at Inchon, and fought along a stable front near Cheorwon. The war involved trench warfare, artillery barrages, and tank support for South Korean infantry. We participated in operations like Task Force Hobart and experienced the challenges of fighting against Chinese forces, eventually settling into a stalemate near the 38th Parallel.

During the Korean War, we supported South Korean troops along the front lines near Cheorwon and Inchon. South Korea was the country we were defending against the North Korean and Chinese forces. In the long run, South Korea became a model democracy and an industrial giant, which is something I witnessed the beginnings of during the conflict.

Task Force Hobart was our first offensive operation into enemy territory during the Korean War. It was a reconnaissance in force named after Hobart, Oklahoma, the hometown of Company C, which was my company. We didn't suffer casualties and it was considered a success, though I almost got court-martialed for writing about it without proper clearance.

Hobart, Oklahoma, was the hometown of Company C, which I commanded during the Korean War. Task Force Hobart was named after this town, linking our military unit to our home community. It was a source of pride and identity for us as soldiers from Oklahoma.

The 38th Parallel was the line along which the Korean War front stabilized. We operated near this line and eventually pulled back to it as part of the armistice negotiations. It became the de facto border between North and South Korea, and we even had a unilateral cease-fire there in April 1952 to test the enemy's response.

China was a major adversary during the Korean War, firing massive artillery barrages and sending troops against us. We faced deadly mortars and mines from Chinese forces, who avoided daylight tank battles but were relentless. The presence of China made the war more complex and dangerous, and their involvement shaped much of the conflict's course.
